name: Double Irish Chain Throw
description: Interlocking chains of color weave across this classic design. Two simple blocks combine to create a pattern that looks far more complex than it is.
summary: Alternate two block types — a 25-patch chain block and a plain block with corner squares — to create the iconic Irish Chain pattern. Strip piecing makes the 25-patch blocks surprisingly fast.

pieces.notes: For center row of chain block

steps.title: Make Strip Set X (Chain Row)
steps.instruction: Sew strips in this order: green, ivory, green, ivory, green. Press all seams toward the green. Make 4 of Strip Set X. Sub-cut each into 2 1/2" segments. You need 48 segments total.

warnings.message: Press consistently toward the green in all strip sets — this enables seam nesting during block assembly.

tips.text: Each WOF strip set yields about 16 segments, so 4 strip sets gives you plenty.

steps.title: Make Strip Set Y (Alternating Row)
steps.instruction: Sew strips in this order: ivory, green, ivory, green, ivory. Press seams toward the green. Make 2 of Strip Set Y. Sub-cut into 2 1/2" segments. You need 24 segments.

steps.title: Make Strip Set Z (Center Row)
steps.instruction: Sew strips: green, ivory, gold, ivory, green. Press seams toward the green and gold. Make 2 of Strip Set Z. Sub-cut into 2 1/2" segments. You need 12 segments.

tips.text: The gold accent in the center row is what gives the Irish Chain its sparkle.

steps.title: Assemble Block A (Chain Block)
steps.instruction: For each Block A, arrange 5 rows: X-Y-Z-Y-X. Sew rows together, nesting seams at every intersection. Press row seams open or in alternating directions. Block should measure 10 1/2" x 10 1/2" unfinished. Make 12 blocks.

warnings.message: All 12 Chain blocks must measure exactly 10 1/2" square. If they don't, check your seam allowance and re-measure.

tips.text: Chain-piece all 12 blocks through each row before pressing.

steps.title: Assemble Block B (Setting Block)
steps.instruction: For each Block B: sew a 2 1/2" green corner square to each end of two 2 1/2" x 6 1/2" ivory rectangles (makes the top and bottom rows). The center row is: ivory rectangle, 6 1/2" ivory center square, ivory rectangle. Sew three rows together. Block measures 10 1/2" x 10 1/2" unfinished. Make 12 blocks.

tips.text: Block B looks plain now, but when set next to Block A, the green corners complete the chain pattern.

steps.title: Arrange Quilt Layout
steps.instruction: Lay out blocks in 6 rows of 4, alternating A and B blocks in a checkerboard. Row 1: A-B-A-B. Row 2: B-A-B-A. The green squares in Block B should align with the chains in Block A to form continuous diagonal chains.

warnings.message: Double-check that the chains connect diagonally before sewing. Rotate blocks if chains don't line up.

tips.text: Step back from your layout and squint — the diagonal chains should be immediately visible.

steps.title: Sew Rows and Join
steps.instruction: Sew blocks into rows, pressing seams toward Block B (which has fewer seams and is flatter). Join rows, nesting seams at block intersections. Press row seams open for a flat quilt top.

tips.text: Pin at every block intersection — the chain alignment depends on these junctions being precise.

steps.title: Add Borders
steps.instruction: Piece border strips end-to-end to get the required lengths. Measure through the quilt center. Add side borders first (should be about 60 1/2"), then top/bottom (about 54 1/2"). Press toward borders.

warnings.message: Piece border strips with diagonal seams to reduce bulk at the joins.

tips.text: If your quilt center measurement differs from the pattern, trust your measurement — your quilt is the authority.

steps.title: Quilt and Bind
steps.instruction: Layer backing, batting, and quilt top. Baste thoroughly — this is a larger quilt and needs secure basting. Quilt with crosshatch lines through the chain, or stitch in the ditch along the chain edges. Trim and bind with 2 1/2" green strips.

tips.text: Crosshatch quilting at 45 degrees echoes the diagonal chain pattern beautifully.
For a throw quilt, machine binding is perfectly acceptable and much faster than hand-stitching.
